title: New York bagels
subtitle: Boiled-then-baked with a chewy crust
excerpt: Bagels with the chewy, dense crumb and shiny crust that comes from boiling in a malt or honey water bath before baking. The boiling step is non-negotiable for the texture — it sets the crust before the oven spring.
sourceNotes: The bagel is an Ashkenazi Jewish bread with roots in sixteenth-century Poland; the New York style was established by Eastern European immigrant bakers from the 1880s onward. The boil-then-bake method, the high-gluten flour, and the relatively lean dough are the defining characteristics of the New York version. Documented in American domestic cookery from the 1950s onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shaped bagels can cold-retard overnight in the fridge; boil and bake from the fridge the next morning, adding 2 minutes to the bake time.

glossaryTerms.definition: The step that defines bagel texture: shaped rings are dropped into simmering water (with honey or malt syrup added) for 30–60 seconds per side before baking. The boil gelatinises the outer starch and sets a dense skin, which produces the characteristic chewy crust and prevents the bagel from rising as dramatically as a standard roll.

content.text: is what separates a bagel from a ring-shaped roll. The dough is stiffer than a standard bread dough (lower hydration) so the bagels hold their ring shape during the boil. The honey or golden syrup in the water contributes to the shiny glaze.
Mix, knead, and bulk ferment, 1 hour

Mix {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{salt-table}}, and {{caster-sugar}}. Add the {{water}} gradually — the dough should be stiff, not sticky. Knead for 10 minutes until very smooth and firm; it should feel noticeably stiffer than a standard bread dough. Cover and bulk ferment for 1 hour until risen but not necessarily doubled.

Divide into 8 pieces (about 100 g each). Shape each into a smooth ball, then poke a finger through the centre and stretch the ring to about 10 cm across. The hole should be generous — it will close up during the boil and bake. Place on a floured tray, cover, and prove for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Line two baking trays with oiled baking paper (not dry parchment, which sticks).

Bring a large, wide pan of {{water}} to a rolling boil. Add the {{golden-syrup}} and {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} if using. Drop the bagels in 2 or 3 at a time — they will sink then float. Boil for 45 seconds per side. Lift out with a slotted spoon, drain briefly, and place on the oiled trays. Top with seeds or toppings of your choice, pressing them in gently. Bake for 20–22 minutes until deep golden with a shiny surface. Cool on a wire rack.
